medications.drug_name: Emollient / thick moisturising hand cream (e.g. white soft paraffin-based or ceramide emollient)
medications.indication_rationale: Restores the skin barrier damaged by hot water and harsh soaps, settling the reactive irritant redness and giving the patient a constructive action to replace the compulsive washing.
Replaces the harsh soaps and sanitisers to stop ongoing irritant injury; lukewarm water only.

medications.drug_name: Broad-spectrum sunscreen SPF 50+ for the depigmented skin
medications.indication_rationale: Depigmented skin has no melanin photoprotection and burns readily; daily broad-spectrum sunscreen on the affected areas prevents sunburn and reduces contrast.

medications.drug_name: Topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor for repigmentation (e.g. a topical steroid or tacrolimus)
medications.indication_rationale: A recognised repigmentation option for vitiligo — but a shared decision for the routine GP/dermatology review after confirmation, NOT started acutely over telehealth. No dose is specified in the source, so none is given here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not to be commenced before in-person confirmation of the diagnosis
Avoid applying to the irritant-inflamed skin until the reactive dermatitis settles

medications.drug_name: Topical or oral antifungal (e.g. terbinafine, clotrimazole)
medications.indication_rationale: There is no clinical evidence of dermatophyte infection (no scale, itch or active border). Prescribing an antifungal to satisfy the patient's contagion fear is an error of commission that endorses a wrong model and delays correct reassurance.
No secondary bacterial infection is present (afebrile, no spreading warmth/swelling). Antibiotics would be unwarranted; they belong only if the RF-001 cellulitis triggers appear.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Acute health anxiety with catastrophic contagion beliefs and compulsive washing warrants psychological support; consider a GP Mental Health Treatment Plan if the anxiety persists beyond this acute episode.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop scrubbing with hot water, harsh soaps and sanitisers immediately; wash gently with lukewarm water and a soap-free cleanser and apply a thick moisturiser instead.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The reactive redness and soreness are self-inflicted by over-washing; stopping it lets the skin settle within days and demonstrates the skin is healthy, not infected.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Name and normalise the panic; use simple grounding/slow-breathing while the reassurance is explained, and plan follow-up support for the health anxiety.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The dominant acute problem is the anxiety; de-escalation prevents an inappropriate emergency presentation and interrupts the compulsive-washing cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen to the depigmented areas whenever exposed to sun.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Protects melanin-deficient skin from sunburn and long-term UV damage.

patient_education_points.point: The white patches are a harmless loss of skin pigment — they are not a fungus, parasite or infection, they are not contagious, and you cannot wash or scrub them away.
The redness and soreness are only from scrubbing your hands raw tonight; it will settle within a few days once you switch to gentle washing and moisturiser.
Wash gently with lukewarm water and a soap-free cleanser, put the harsh soaps and sanitisers away, and moisturise.
Use broad-spectrum sunscreen on the white areas daily — that skin has no natural protection against sunburn.
This does not need an emergency department tonight; a routine GP or dermatology appointment in the next few weeks can confirm it and discuss the natural history and treatment options.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP or dermatology review in 2–3 weeks
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm the diagnosis by in-person exam (± Wood's lamp), formally exclude leprosy with sensation testing, check the irritant dermatitis has settled, review the health anxiety, and discuss natural history and topical repigmentation options.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Skin breaking down into open sores, spreading warmth/swelling or fever (possible secondary infection), a new thick crusty or itchy scale over the patches, or worsening/uncontrollable anxiety.

must_include_items: Reassure that the finding is benign and non-contagious — not a fungal/parasitic infection and cannot be washed away
Explicitly recognise and de-escalate the acute anxiety / panic
Instruct the patient to stop the harsh washing and switch to gentle cleanser + moisturiser
Arrange routine in-person GP/dermatology review (2–3 weeks) rather than emergency escalation
Provide clear safety-net triggers for secondary infection
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending daily broad-spectrum sunscreen to the depigmented skin
Offering a GP Mental Health Treatment Plan / psychology referral for the health anxiety
errors_of_omission: Failing to address or de-escalate the panic and obsessive washing
Not advising cessation of harsh soaps/hot water
Not giving any safety-net for secondary infection
Not arranging any follow-up to confirm the diagnosis / exclude leprosy
errors_of_commission: Prescribing an antifungal (or antibiotic) for an infection that is not present
Escalating a benign cosmetic finding to the emergency department in response to the patient's panic
Declaring the diagnosis definitively confirmed over telehealth without noting that in-person exam and sensation testing are needed
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES benign reassurance + anxiety de-escalation + stopping the harsh washing + a routine (not emergency) follow-up. Escalating to the ED, or prescribing an antifungal/antibiotic, is a scored error of commission.
